problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem B (discrete) on the Colorado River. The team had to (a) given initial water levels of Lake Powell (Glen Canyon Dam) and Lake Mead (Hoover Dam), determine how much water to draw from each lake to meet fixed water + hydroelectric demand of 5 states (AZ, CA, WY, NM, CO); (b) find the maximum number of days the fixed demand can be sustained with no additional inflow; (c) compute the additional water needed over time to keep meeting demand; (d) allocate water/electricity across industrial, agricultural, and residential users in the 5 states with criteria to resolve competing interests; (e) extend to the water-shortage case and to Mexico's share; and (f) run sensitivity analysis on demand changes, renewable-energy tech, and conservation. Note: the actual 2022 Problem B was about asteroid mining / sequestering value — this team's paper is a Colorado-River water-allocation problem, so the corpus label and the paper content concern reservoir water/electricity allocation.
models_used: Water level to storage-volume relationship via quadratic (2nd-degree) polynomial curve fitting in MATLAB, validated against Wikipedia capacity figures (v_powell = 224.26 l^2 - 1.5157e6 l + 2.5622e9; v_mead = 338.64 l^2 - 6.719e5 l + 3.4155e8)

Model I: Water and Electricity Transportation Model — a linear-programming transportation model minimizing total water supply W_supply = sum_i sum_j W_ij subject to reservoir-stock caps (W1<=Q_G, W2<=Q_H+gamma*lambda*W1down) and per-state water + electricity demand constraints

Hydroelectric power conversion: turbine power formula E = (eta+beta)(rho*Vdot)g*Δh*Δt converting reservoir potential energy to electricity, with generation head Δh = current level − minimum generating level
Electricity transmission loss model E_loss = I^2 * rho' * L_ij * Δt / s (resistive line loss as function of distance), folded back into an equivalent extra water draw W_eloss
Evaporation/transport water-loss model W_wloss = W_ij * alpha^(sigma/(T*d_ij)) — temperature- and distance-dependent loss coefficient

Maintenance Time Calculation Algorithm — a custom iterative/greedy day-by-day simulation that repeatedly re-solves Model I, decrements reservoir volume, recomputes water level from the fitting curve, and counts days until both levels fall below the minimum generating level (answer: 63 days)

Model II: Coordinated Development Multi-objective Programming Model — opt[f1,f2,f3] over Social effect (minimize unmet demand), Economic effect (maximize benefit), Environmental effect (minimize pollutant discharge, COD/BOD), with min/max demand and pollutant-cap constraints

Weighted-coefficient priority system inside the economic objective: order coefficient chi (source priority by distance), fairness coefficient delta (industry/agri/residential priority), regional tradeoff coefficient w (per-state weights from real usage data), each computed via a normalized rank-to-weight transform chi = (1 + n_max - n)/sum(1 + n_max - n)
Guarantee-rate parametrization (50%/75%/95%) to trace the Pareto trade-off among the three objectives under water shortage

Mexican Allocation Model — a Logistic-curve tiered allocation mu(t) = 1/(1 + (mu_m/mu_0 - 1) e^(-rt)) mapping surplus ratio Rem/Req to Mexico's share (mu~0 when surplus small, mu~1 when surplus >= 2x monthly US demand)
key_techniques: Reducing a physically messy problem to a clean LP by converting everything into one currency — water volume: electricity demand and transmission losses are all re-expressed as equivalent water draw via the turbine power formula, so a single transportation LP can be solved

Polynomial fitting as a reusable lookup function: the level to volume quadratic is fit once, then reused inside the day-by-day maintenance algorithm to translate volume back to head height each iteration

Layered modeling: Model I (state-level supply) is explicitly extended into Model II (user-level multi-objective) and then Model III (Mexico), so each model reuses the prior model's constraints and outputs rather than starting over

Encoding qualitative priorities (residential > agricultural > industrial water; Hoover-before-Glen by distance) as quantitative normalized rank weights (order/fairness/regional coefficients) that plug directly into the economic objective
Using guarantee rate as a single knob to generate a Pareto-style trade-off table among social/economic/environmental objectives instead of arbitrarily weighting them
Borrowing the Logistic curve from population/growth modeling to get a smooth, bounded, interpretable surplus-to-allocation rule for Mexico

Validation by back-prediction: checking the fitted level to volume curves against independently sourced Wikipedia active-capacity numbers, and sanity-checking that a day's draw changes the level by only ~0.45 / 1.55 ft
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness and physical defensibility rather than mathematical sophistication. It answers every sub-question of a sprawling open prompt (per-lake draw, 63-day maintenance horizon, additional-water curve vs temperature, three-user allocation, shortage case, Mexico, and three sensitivity axes) — judges reward papers that close the loop on the whole problem. It grounds an under-specified problem with real collected data (lake water-data sites, EIA electricity data, state usage weights) and cites it, which signals diligence on a no-data problem. The single best move is unifying water and electricity into one transportation LP by converting power and line losses into equivalent water — this is an elegant simplification that keeps the model solvable while staying physically motivated. Model II's social/economic/environmental decomposition with a guarantee-rate trade-off table directly addresses the 'resolve competing interests' clause the prompt asks for, and the explicit observation that improving economic effect costs social and environmental effect shows the team understands trade-offs, not just optimization. Clear two-model + extension structure, a named custom algorithm with pseudocode, and an honest strengths/weaknesses section round out a professional, judge-friendly package."
section_highlights: "Strong structural choices: (1) A dedicated Model Preparation section (4.2) that derives the level to volume fitting before any model, so later sections can treat it as a black-box function — good dependency ordering. (2) Clear Model I -> Model II -> Mexico layering with a 'this model is a further extension of' framing that makes the escalation legible. (3) The Maintenance Time Calculation Algorithm is presented with explicit input/output and pseudocode (Section 5.3), which reads as a concrete deliverable rather than hand-waving. (4) A consistent notation table up front (Section 3) with heavily superscripted/subscripted symbols x_ij^km that are reused verbatim in both models. (5) Worked parameter section (6.2) that uses California as a single fully-worked example to justify every coefficient (benefit, charging, order, fairness, regional) instead of dumping all 5 states — smart space management explicitly acknowledged ('Due to the limitation of paper space'). (6) Three-axis sensitivity analysis (demand +/-5%, beta renewable uplift 5/10/15%, conservation) each with a chart and an interpretation of WHY the result moves. (7) A genuinely self-critical weaknesses section (missing parameter values; hydro-only generation). (8) A standalone 1-2 page magazine article as required, written in accessible prose."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-invented, lightly-justified parameters: alpha, sigma, gamma, lambda in the loss models and the Logistic mu_m/mu_0/r = 9.2 are fit or assumed with little empirical grounding — the team admits some specific parameter values were never found

Geography/flow direction error: the paper states 'Lake Mead has a higher elevation than Lake Powell' yet also says water flows Powell -> Mead -> Gulf; Lake Powell (Glen Canyon, ~3,600+ ft) is actually far higher than Lake Mead (~1,200 ft). The directional logic is right but the elevation claim is contradictory/wrong

Only hydroelectric generation is modeled (assumption stated), so the electricity side is unrealistic for states like California whose grid is mostly non-hydro; demand numbers and allocations are correspondingly soft
State list is inconsistent with the real basin: the paper uses AZ, CA, WY, NM, CO and omits Utah and Nevada (Hoover serves Las Vegas), which undercuts realism of the 5-state allocation

Data plausibility is shaky in places: the embedded chart tables (e.g., level/content axes, the additional-water vs temperature figure) have rough or rounded values, and the drought heatmap table appears garbled/auto-extracted

The multi-objective program is never shown being solved with a stated method (no genetic algorithm / weighting / epsilon-constraint procedure detailed despite citing GA and SA references) — only result tables and a guarantee-rate sweep are given, so reproducibility of f1/f2/f3 numbers is limited
Units are inconsistent across the paper (gallons vs acre-feet vs million gallons; KMWh/KWh typos), which a stricter judge would flag
The Maintenance Time pseudocode mixes alpha/beta as loss fractions in a way that doesn't cleanly match their earlier definitions, so the 63-day figure rests on an under-specified loop
reusable_patterns: Common-currency reduction: when a problem couples two resources (here water + electricity), convert one into the other via a physical law (turbine formula) so a single optimization model covers both — directly reusable for any coupled-resource allocation problem

Fit-once-reuse-many: derive an empirical relationship (level to volume) by polynomial fitting in a prep section, validate it against independent ground truth, then call it as a function inside an iterative time-stepping algorithm

Layered model escalation: Model I solves the coarse version, Model II adds disaggregation (users) and extra objectives reusing Model I's constraints, Model III handles a special actor (Mexico) — a clean template for multi-part MCM prompts

Rank-to-weight normalization: turn any ordinal priority ('residential before industrial', 'nearer dam first') into a normalized coefficient via (1 + n_max - n)/sum(...) and multiply it into the objective — a generic way to encode soft preferences quantitatively
Guarantee-rate / service-level sweep to expose a Pareto trade-off table among competing objectives instead of committing to fixed weights — strong way to demonstrate trade-offs to judges

Logistic curve as a smooth bounded allocation/switching function: use mu(t)=1/(1+((mu_m/mu_0)-1)e^{-rt}) to map a surplus ratio to a 0-to-1 share with a tunable midpoint — reusable wherever you need a soft threshold rule
Single worked example to control length: fully derive all coefficients for one entity (California) and state explicitly that the rest follow identically — saves pages while proving the method

Sensitivity analysis that explains the mechanism: for each perturbation, state the direction of change AND the physical reason (e.g., evaporation loss dominates water-use-efficiency gains), which reads far stronger than a bare table
